content.full_text:
Farn PolrrrcaL Pnlcucns ConaurssroN
P.O. Box tt07 . 428 J Street . Sacramento, CA 9-5til2-0tt07
(916) 322-.5660 . Fax (916) 322-0titt6
August 7,2000
Scott Smith
City Attorney, City of Santee
Best Best & Krieger
402 West Broadway, 13ft Floor
San Diego, California 92101-3542
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-153
Dear Mr. Smith:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Santee Mayor Jack Dale
about the conflict-of-interist provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r
QUESTION
Mayor Dale owns stock in Country Music Marathon, a corporation that owns a race event
in Missouri. Country Music Marathon contracts with Elite Racing to operate the Missouri event.
May Mayor Dale participate in a city council decision to provide funding to Elite Racing for the
operation of another racing event that will take place in Santee?
CONCLUSION
No. Mayor Dale has a conflict of interest in the decision based on his economic interest
in Elite Racing, a business entity that is otherwise related to Country Music Marathon.
FACTS
Elite Racing Inc. is a sports management and marketing company. It owns, operates and
trademarked the Steve Scott Festival of Races, an annual event held in the City of Santee in past
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear attitle2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-00-153
Page2
yeils. From time to time, Elite Racing approaches Santee with funding requests in the interest of
promoting the city. In the past, the city has providing funding to Elite Racing ranging from
$10,000 to $20,000. Last year, the city did not provide any funding to Elite Racing. This lack of
funding was central to the failure of last year's race festival.
Elite Racing is a private corporation, comprised of one majority shareholder and several
smaller ones. Elite Racing has,approached the city for funding as part of this year's budget
process. Mayor Dale does not own stock in Elite Racing. But he does own stock in another
corporation, the Country Music Marathon ("CMM"). CMM owns the right to a race event in
Branson, Missouri, an event similar to the annual festival in Santee. CMM contracts with Elite
Racing to operate the Branson event.
Elite Racing and CMM are not in a parent-subsidiary relationship. CMM merely hires
Elite Racing as an event manager. CMM only owns the right to one event. In contrast, Elite
Racing owns its own event and operates about a dozen events each year owned by other entities.
Neither entity has a controlling ownership in the other. In addition, the two businesses do not
have common or commingled funds or assets, or share the same resources or personnel. The
same person currently has a majority ownership in both entities. However, CMM is in the
process of acquiring new investors. Once the investment process is over, the majority
shareholder with Elite Racing will no longer have a majority ownership interest in CMM.
ANALYSIS
A. Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition
A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his or
her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official knows, or has
reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) The Commission has
developed an eighrstep approach for determining whether an individual has a disqualiffing
financial interest in a decision. (Regulation 18700(b).)
1. Public Official
The conflict-of-interest prohibition only applies to public officials. (Section 87100.) The
Mayor of Santee is a public official for purposes of the Act. (Section 82048.)
2. Conduct Covered
The prohibition covers specific conduct: making, participating in making, or attempting
to use one's official position to influence a govemmental decision. (Section 87100.) These
terms are defined in Regulations 18702-18702.4. By approving funding for the race festival, the
mayor will be engaging in activity regulated by the Act.


File No. 4-00-153
Page 3
3. Identifying Relevant Economic Interests
A public official has a disqualiffing financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on the official,2 or on the following enumerated economic interests:
l. Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth
$1,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).)
2. ky real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b).)
3. Any source of income of $250 or more provided to the official within 12 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(c).)
4. ky business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)
5. Any donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the official within l2 months
before the decision. (Section 87103(e).)
Mayor Dale has an investment interest in CMM that is presumably worth $1,000 or more.
(Section 87103(a).) Therefore, he has an economic interest in CMM. Mayor Dale may also
have an economic interest in Elite Racing.
a. Economic Interest in Otherwise Related Business Entities
CMM hires Elite Racing to organize the annual race event in Missouri that it owns.
(Section 87103(a).) The mayor does not own stock in Elite Racing. However, when an official
has an investment interest in one business entity, the official also has an economic interest in any
business entity that is either a parent or subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, that business
entity. (Regulation 18703.1(c).)
CMM and Elite Racing are not in a parent/subsidiary relationship. Such a relationship
exists when one corporation directly or indirectly owns shares possessing more than 50 percent
of the voting power of another corporation. (Regulation 18703.1(dxl).) But the two entities
may be otherwise related.
2 A decision will have a financial effect "on the official," within the meaning of Section 87103, if the
decision will increase or decrease the official's personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those of his or her
immediate family. (Regulation 18703.5.) This is known as the "personal financial effect" rule. When determining
whether the rule applies in a given situation, we do not consider the financial effects on a business entity in which
an official has an investrnent interest. (IDld.) Since the only economic interest you describe is the mayor's interest
in a business entity, the personal financial effect rule does not apply.


File No. A-00-153
Page 4
Two business entities are "otherwise related" if any one of three tests is met. Under the
first test, one business entity must have a controlling ownership interest in the other business
entity. Neither CMM nor Elite Racing have an ownership interest in the other. (Regulation
18703.1(d)(2XA).)
The second test requires that there be shared management and control between the two
entities. (Regulation 18703.1(d)(2XB).) To make this determination, four criteria must be
considered: (l) whether the same person or substantially the same person owns and manages the
two entities; (2) whether there are common or commingled funds or assets; (3) whether the
business entities share the use of the same offices or employees, or otherwise share activities,
resources or personnel on a regular basis; and (a) whether there is otherwise a regular and close
working relationship between the entities. (Ibid.) Applying the fourth factor, it appears that a
"regular and close working relationship" may exist between Elite Racing and CMM. However,
this factor is but one of four, and none of the other three factors applies to your facts. Therefore,
we cannot conclude under the second test that the two corporations are otherwise related.
Under the third test, when a controlling owner (50 percent or greater interest as a
shareholder or as a general partner) in one entity is also a controlling owner in the other entity,
the two entities are otherwise related. (Regulation 18703.1(d)(2XC).) You mention that the one
person owns a controlling ownership interest in both CMM and Elite Racing. Accordingly,
CMM and Elite Racing are otherwise related business entities under the third test. As such,
Mayor Dale has an economic interest in Elite Racing for purposes of the Act.3
Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official must
evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial effect
on one or more of those economic interests. It does not appear that the decision at issue will
have any effect on CMM. Therefore, we will only focus on the mayor's economic interest in
Elite Racing to determine whether a conflict exists.
This determination takes three steps, which are discussed as Steps 4,5, and 6. First, the
official must determine whether the economic interest will be directly or indirectly involved in
the decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) Based upon the type of involvement, the official must
then find the applicable materiality standard set forth in Commission regulations. (Regulation
18700(bX5).) After finding the applicable materiality standard, the official must then decide
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Regulation 18700(b)(6).)
3 However, when the recapitalization process is complete and the majority shareholder of Elite Racing no
longer has a majority ownership interest in Elite Racing, then Mayor Dale will not have an economic interest in
Elite Racing for purposes of the Act.


File No. A-00-153
Page 5
4. Direct or Indirect Involvement
A person, including a business entity, is directly involved in a decision if the person
initiates, is a named party in, or the subject of the decision. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).) As a
party requesting funding in the city's budget process, Elite Racing is directly involved in the
city's decision to approve that funding.
5. Applicable Materiality Standard
In general, when a business entity in which an official has an investment interest is
directly involved in a decision, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the decision is
deemed to be material.a (Regulation 18705.1(a).) Mayor Dale's investment interest in CMM is
imputed to Elite Racing since the businesses are otherwise related. (Warden Advice Letter, No.
A-94-20I.) Therefore, Regulation 18705.1(a), which applies to investment interests in business
entities, is the applicable materiality standard.
6. Foreseeability
A financial effect is considered reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental
decision is made if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706.) A
material financial effect need not be a certainty as a result of the decision, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Consequently, the question becomes whether it is substantially likely that the city's
decision to approve funding for the race festival will have any financial effect on Elite Racing. A
decision granting money to Elite Racing will have some financial effect on that company. As
such, Mayor Dale has a conflict of interest in that funding decision.
7. Public Generally Exception
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still participate in
the decision if the "public generally" exception applies. (Section 87103.) For this exception to
apply, the decision must affect the official's economic interests in substantially the same manner
as it would affect a significant segment of the public. (Regulation 18707.) For decisions that
affect a business entity, a "significant segment" of the public is 50 percent of all businesses in the
jurisdiction or the district the official represents. (Regulation 18707(bxlXB).) Fifty percent of
the businesses in the City of Santee will not be affected by the city's decision to fund Elite
Racing. Therefore, the public generally exception does not apply.
a The exception to the materiality standard for investrnent interests in large, publicly-traded companies
does not appear to apply to your facts. (See Regulation 18705.1(a).)


File No. A-00-153
Page 6
8. Legally Required Participation
The eighth step is the "legally required participation" rule. (See Regulation 18708.) This
rule only applies when several public officials in the same agency are simultaneously
disqualified. The legally required participation rule does not appear to be relevant to your facts.
Accordingly, Mayor Dale may not participate in the city's decision to approve funding
for the annual race event operated by Elite Racing.
B. Segmenting Decisions
Although Mayor Dale may not participate in the decision to approve funding for Elite
Racing, he may be able to participate in other aspects of the city's budget. A public body may
divide large and complex decisions, like budget decisions, into separate ones in order to allow an
official, who has a conflict in one area, to participate in other parts of the decision.
The Commission has devised a procedure that must be followed if the mayor wishes to
provide input on other items in the budget. (Huffaker Advice Letter, No. ,4.-86-343.) First, the
line item in which he has a conflict must be considered separately. That item must be considered
first, and a final decision must be reached. Finally, once a final decision has been made on that
item, the mayor may participate in the other budget items, as long as those deliberations do not
reopen or in any way affect the item in which he has a conflict.
In addition to participating in other aspects of the budget, the mayor may also vote on the
adoption of the final budget. He may do this even though the final budget includes the item from
which he was disqualified, provided that the decision does not reopen or in any way alter the
decision from which he was disqualified. (CookAdvice Letter, No. 4-83-163.)
If you have other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel
w
By: Julia Bilaver
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

QUESTION: Mayor Dale owns stock in Country Music Marathon, a corporation that owns a race event
in Missouri. Country Music Marathon contracts with Elite Racing to operate the Missouri event.
May Mayor Dale participate in a city council decision to provide funding to Elite Racing for the
operation of another racing event that will take place in Santee?

CONCLUSION: No. Mayor Dale has a conflict of interest in the decision based on his economic interest
in Elite Racing, a business entity that is otherwise related to Country Music Marathon.
